diagrams.description: This timeline traces the historical development of Developmental Psychology. Early scientific observations began with Darwin’s baby biography (1877) and Preyer’s 'The Mind of the Child' (1882). The early 20th century saw Binet & Simon’s intelligence scale (1905) and Gesell’s maturational theory (1920s). Piaget’s revolutionary work on cognitive stages emerged in the 1930s‑40s, while Vygotsky’s sociocultural theory (1934) gained Western recognition later. Attachment theory was established by Bowlby (1951) and Ainsworth (1978). Erikson’s psychosocial stages (1950) extended development across the lifespan. Bronfenbrenner’s ecological model (1979) emphasized context. The late 20th century brought developmental neuroscience (1990s) and the rise of lifespan developmental science (Baltes, 1987). Recent work integrates epigenetics and global perspectives (2010s‑2020s).
